rationale: Title X names both trial arms (catheter ablation versus medical therapy), which is essential for characterizing this as a comparative RCT and is precisely the kind of design JACC prioritizes. It also delivers the key finding directly — transient decline with full recovery at 12 months — giving readers the bottom line without sacrificing scientific precision. Title Y omits the medical-therapy comparator entirely, making the study appear as a single-arm observational report rather than an RCT, which misrepresents the study design and reduces its perceived novelty. Y also uses 'Transient and Reversible' redundantly (transient implies reversibility) and replaces the specific finding with the vague phrase 'Cognitive Outcomes,' losing the informative punch that JACC titles require.
loser_weakness: Title Y drops the comparator arm (medical therapy), making the RCT design invisible; the redundant 'transient and reversible' phrasing wastes words; and 'Cognitive Outcomes Through 12 Months' conveys no finding, leaving readers uninformed about the study result.
